paper_title: FlexiSLM: A Dynamic and Controllable Frame Rate Spoken Language Model

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Spoken language models (SLMs) extend LLMs to speech input and output. Existing SLMs represent speech at fixed frame rates (e.g., 25 or 12.5 Hz), ignoring the time-varying information density of speech and offering no flexibility to trade off quality for speed at inference time. Recent audio tokenizer research has proposed dynamic frame rate speech coding, which exploits this non-uniformity and enables two new capabilities: very low average frame rates and frame rate controllability. However, this technique has not yet been applied to SLMs. We introduce Flexible Spoken Language Model (FlexiSLM), the first SLM that supports dynamic and controllable frame rates on both speech input and output. Using dynamic frame rate representations, FlexiSLM outperforms fixed-frame-rate 7B models including Qwen2.5-Omni and Kimi-Audio at its high-quality operating points. We further verify that FlexiSLM can be accurately steered down to 4.0 Hz; at 6.25 Hz, it roughly halves inference time relative to 12.5 Hz while retaining strong speech-to-speech quality. Audio samples are available at https://flexislm.github.io .

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Spoken language models (SLMs) have emerged as unified framework for speech understanding and generation, covering speech-to-speech dialogue, automatic speech recognition (ASR), text-tospeech (TTS), and audio understanding (Xu et al., 2025a; Ding et al., 2025; Zeng et al., 2024; Défossez et al., 2024). These models jointly model text and speech with large language model (LLM) backbone, but typically represent speech at fixed frame rate1, e.g., 25 Hz for Qwen2.5-Omni (Xu Qwen3-Omni-30B Fun-Audio-Chat-8B GLM 4-Voice-9B Mimo-Audio-7B Kimi-Audio-7B Qwen2.5-Omni-7B 12.5 25(5.0) 12.5 25(6.25) 12.5 25 in / 50 out BPE Text Tokens 4.5 FlexiSLM-7B 4.0 12.5 - - Table 1: Capability comparison with representative spoken language models. FR denotes the frame rate of each systems input and output speech representations. et al., 2025a) and 12.5 Hz for Kimi-Audio (Ding et al., 2025). Fixed-rate tokenization ignores the time-varying information density of speech, wasting compute on silences and other informationsparse segments. It also prevents inference-time qualityspeed control across devices, networks, and deployment budgets. FlexiCodec (Li et al., 2025b) addresses these limitations with dynamic frame rate codec that uses frame merging to achieve strong audio reconstruction quality at an average of 6.25 Hz, while allowing the average frame rate to be steered at inference time. Figure 1 illustrates this concept. However, FlexiCodec was validated only in 0.3Bparameter TTS pipeline. Extending dynamic-rate coding to end-to-end SLMs is more challenging, but also more valuable: SLMs are more computeintensive, and their broader capability set makes frame rate controllability useful for heterogeneous deployment budgets. Motivated by these benefits, we develop Flexible Spoken Language Model (FlexiSLM), the first spoken language model with dynamic and controllable Corresponding to: wuzhizheng@cuhk.edu.cn jiaqili3@link.cuhk.edu.cn; 1The frame rate is the number of discrete or continuous speech-encoding frames used to represent one second of audio; lower frame rates use fewer tokens. These systems use patching, yielding effective LLM-side frame rates of 5 Hz for Fun-Audio-Chat and 6.25 Hz for MimoAudio. This approach is complementary to our dynamic frame rate-based compression, and we leave the combination of both strategies to future work. tent, and are increasingly used in speech language modeling (Borsos et al., 2023; Du et al., 2024a; Ding et al., 2025). Recent work has pushed toward more efficient representations while maintaining high audio quality: single-codebook approaches (5075 Hz) (Wu et al., 2024; Ji et al., 2024) and semantic-enhanced codecs (12.550 Hz) (Li et al., 2025a; Zhang et al., 2023). Recent work has explored dynamic frame rates, leveraging the temporal sparsity of speech so that lower average frame rates reduce the computational cost of speech language models. FlexiCodec (Li et al., 2025b), the tokenizer used in this work, merges 12.5 Hz semantic features based on similarity to achieve dynamic-rate tokenization at an average of 6.25 Hz. The authors of FlexiCodec also demonstrate controllable frame rate tokenization and TTS. Other dynamic frame rate works, including CodecSlime (Wang et al., 2025), TFC (Zhang et al., 2025b), and VARSTok (Zheng et al., 2025), explore higher average frame rates from 18.75 Hz to 40 Hz. However, dynamic-rate codecs have not been applied within spoken language model framework, where low frame rates and frame rate controllability offer larger practical benefits. Spoken Language Models. Spoken language models (SLMs) are general-purpose speech processing systems (Arora et al., 2025). Analogous to text LLMs, they can follow natural-language instructions across diverse speech tasks. Recent end-to-end SLMs extend text-based LLMs to directly comprehend and generate speech. dominant design follows three-stage paradigm: pretrained speech encoder extracts acoustic features, which condition decoder-only LLM, followed by an additional transformer module or prediction head that predicts speech tokens (Wang et al., 2026; Xu et al., 2025a; Ding et al., 2025). Kimi-Audio (Ding et al., 2025) models parallel speech-text at 12.5 Hz using separate LM head for speech tokens. Qwen2.5-Omni (Xu et al., 2025a) adopts thinker talker architecture operating at 25 Hz, whose talker module predicts speech tokens. Active research areas include full-duplex capability (e.g., Interaction Models (Défossez et al., 2024)), interleaved speech-text sequences (e.g., GLM-4-Voice (Zeng et al., 2024)), low-frame-rate audio tokenization for SLMs (e.g., Moshi (Défossez et al., 2024)), and dual-resolution speech representations (e.g., Fun-Audio-Chat/DrVoice (Tan et al., 2025b,a) and Mimo-Audio (Zhang et al., 2025a) group speech Figure 1: high-level illustration of the dynamic frame rate strategy we use. The frame merging module adaptively compresses speech based on information density. frame rates. FlexiSLM is thinker-talker speech-in, speech-out model: for output, we reuse FlexiCodec as the talker prediction target; for input, we apply similar frame-merging strategy to continuous speech representations. For controllable generation, we introduce conditioning signal that lets the user directly specify the average output frame rate, allowing one FlexiSLM to operate at any frame rate 12.5 Hz without retraining. Our contributions are summarized as follows: Dynamic frame rate SLM framework and validation. We introduce FlexiSLM, the first dynamic frame rate SLM framework, with dynamic frame compression on both speech input and output. Experiments show strong performance at 12.5 Hz and 6.25 Hz, with graceful degradation at 5.0 Hz and 4.0 Hz. We plan to release our code2, and reproduced data and model to support future research. Accurate and practical frame rate control. We propose direct frame rate conditioning, letting users specify the average output frame rate instead of indirectly tuning merging threshold. This makes FlexiSLM, to our knowledge, the first SLM with frame rate controllability.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Speech Tokenization. Speech tokenization converts continuous audio into discrete tokens suitable for speech language modeling. Early neural audio codecs such as SoundStream (Zeghidour et al., 2021) and EnCodec (Défossez et al., 2022) use residual vector quantization (RVQ) to produce acoustic tokens at fixed frame rates (e.g., 50 Hz or 75 Hz), prioritizing reconstruction fidelity. Semantic tokens derived from self-supervised models like HuBERT (Hsu et al., 2021) capture linguistic con2Code will be released at https://github.com/ AmphionTeam/FlexiSLM. Figure 2: Overall architecture of FlexiSLM. into 5 Hz or 6.25 Hz sequences).

sections.title: 3.1 Architecture Overview
sections.content: Figure 2 illustrates the overall architecture of FlexiSLM, parallel speech-text model with the following components: Audio Encoder. This module encodes the users speech into semantic-rich continuous representation suitable for LLM understanding. We adopt the pretrained Qwen2.5-Omni audio encoder (Xu et al., 2025a), which extracts 25 Hz continuous speech features from waveforms. Frame Merging Module. This module compresses the number of frames in sequence. It appears twice in our model: (1) on the input side, it reduces the 25 Hz continuous features from the Audio Encoder to dynamic-rate sequence 12.5 Hz; (2) inside the pretrained FlexiCodec audio tokenizer, it merges 12.5 Hz ASR features before quantization. Both instances share the same merging mechanism. We describe this module in Section 3.2. LLM Backbone (Thinker). We use Qwen2.5-7BInstruct (Yang et al., 2024) as initialization. This LLM has also been used in Qwen2.5-Omni and Kimi-Audio to initialize their backbones. FlexiCodec Audio Tokenizer. We use the opensource pretrained FlexiCodec to obtain discrete speech tokens as the prediction target of FlexiSLMs Talker module. As illustrated in Figure 2(a), the codec discretizes each frame with Finite Scalar Quantization (FSQ (Mentzer et al., 2023)); each token is paired with frame length attribute for audio reconstruction. We use FlexiCodecs semantic tokens and omit its RVQ acoustic tokens. Appendix provides more details. Talker Transformer. The Talker decodes the Thinker LLMs hidden states and outputs into FlexiCodecs dynamic-frame-rate speech tokens. Input: As shown in Figure 3, its input sequence runs over the entire user + assistant context. At each position, the Talker input embedding is projected from the concatenation of (1) the backbone LLMs last-layer hidden state, (2) sinusoidal embedding of the target frame rate (Section 3.3), and (3) the embeddings of the previously emitted speech and frame length tokens. Output: The Talker produces two parallel output streams: FlexiCodec FSQ codes and their associated frame lengths, enabling dynamic-rate output. The Talker uses two output LM heads to predict the streams in parallel. Token delay: As shown in Figure 3, the Talkers FSQ token stream is delayed by 5 tokens from the text stream. This provides small lookahead that prevents speech from preceding its corresponding text (Du et al., 2024b). The frame length tokens are delayed by an additional one position, allowing the model to predict frames duration after knowing its corresponding speech token (Li et al., 2025b). Audio Decoder. The audio decoder is frozen non-autoregressive (NAR) flow-matching (Lipman Figure 3: Talker Transformer input-output structure. et al., 2022) Transformer that decodes mel spectrograms from the speech tokens. Vocos (Siuzdak, 2023) neural vocoder then converts the mel spectrogram into 24 kHz speech. We use the pretrained flow-matching model and vocoder from the open-source FlexiCodec repository. We provide additional details in Appendix G. Talker-to-Thinker Connection. In addition to the standard cascaded Thinker-to-Talker information flow (Xu et al., 2025a), FlexiSLM contains an optional Talker-to-Thinker connection (Tan et al., 2025a) (the red dashed arrow in Figure 2) that feeds the Talkers previously emitted speech-token embeddings back into the Thinker LLM Backbone at the next step. This gives the Thinker LLM explicit access to what has already been spoken. The connection projects the concatenation of the Talkers embeddings (speech code + frame length) and the text embedding into the Thinker LLMs hidden state. We can disable this connection by zeroing out the contribution of the Talkers embeddings in the concatenation.

sections.title: 3.2 Frame Merging Module
sections.content: As shown in Figure 2(b), the Frame Merging Module adaptively compresses fixed-rate semantic feature sequence by merging adjacent frames that carry redundant information. Given sequence of feature vectors x1, x2, . . . , xT at base frame rate, we compute the cosine similarity between consecutive frames: st = xtxt+1 = 1, . . . , 1. xt xt+1 , If st exceeds merging threshold τ , frames xt and xt+1 are grouped and their average is computed. This process runs greedily from left to right, with contiguous high-similarity frames merged into single averaged representation. terleave the original and averaged features to form an augmented sequence, which is processed by lightweight Transformer with local attention. Finally, we retrieve the representations at positions corresponding to the averaged features, yielding the merged sequence with associated frame lengths.

sections.title: 3.3 Controllable Frame Rate
sections.content: A key feature of FlexiSLM is its ability to control the output frame rate at inference time, enabling single deployed model to operate across range of compute budgets without retraining. For dynamic frame rate sequence, the average frame rate is defined as Total number of frames after merging . We first describe baseline threshold-based strategy and its limitations, then introduce our proposed direct frame rate control. We focus on output frame rate control; input frame rate is controlled by computing the target number of merged frames from the desired rate and selecting merging threshold per utterance to match it. Audio duration in seconds Merging Threshold Control (Baseline). straightforward approach is to control the merging threshold τ : higher τ merges fewer frames (higher frame rate), while lower τ merges more (lower frame rate). This approach is used in FlexiCodec-TTS (Li et al., 2025b). However, it provides only indirect control, with several limitations: (1) the resulting frame rate varies significantly across utterances and datasets, making the speedup difficult to predict  (Table 4)  ; (2) it is one-to-many mapping: single threshold maps to wide distribution of average frame rates, increasing modeling complexity; and (3) it is unintuitive for users unfamiliar with the model architecture. After merging, each group produces an averaged feature xk and frame length attribute lk denoting the number of original frames in the group. We inDirect Frame-Rate Control. During training, we randomly sample merging thresholds, compute the resulting average frame rate for each utterance, and feed this empirical rate as conditioning signal. At inference, the user simply specifies the desired frame rate. To over enable control continuous range of frame rates, we encode the scalar frame rate using sinusoidal positional enPE(r) = coding (Vaswani et al., 2017): [sin(rω1), cos(rω1), . . . , sin(rωd), cos(rωd)], where ωi = 10,000 are frequency bases. This encoding serves as one of the inputs for Talker. Each position in the talker input sequence receives the same frame rate condition.

sections.title: 3.4 Training Procedure
sections.content: Training proceeds in three stages: Stage 1: Talker Pre-training. In this stage, we freeze the LLM backbone and train only the randomly initialized Talker. We train FlexiSLM end to end with TTS data. This stage uses English samples from Emilia (He et al., 2024) and Multilingual LibriSpeech (MLS) (Pratap et al., 2020), totaling about 100K training hours. The Talker-to-Thinker connection is disabled in Stages 1 and 2. Stage 2: Multi-Task LoRA Fine-Tuning. In this stage, we activate the speech input-side Frame Merging Module, Thinker, and Talker. LoRA (Hu et al., 2022) is applied to the Thinker. The Frame Merging Module is randomly initialized. We train on mixture of speech processing tasks. Table 2 shows the distribution of the data sources in Stage 2. central component is FlexiSLM-Data, speech-to-speech dialogue corpus constructed for this work. FlexiSLM-Data is distilled from 30B Qwen3-Omni (Xu et al., 2025b) SLM, with data sourced from wide range of datasets. We detail Stage 3: Full Fine-Tuning. This stage further unlocks the backbones capacity and improves speech perception. It continues from the Stage 2 checkpoint and uses the same data, with two differences. First, we perform full-parameter training after merging the LoRA updates back into the target LLM weight matrices. Second, we enable and train the Talker-to-Thinker connection.

sections.title: 3.5 Training Loss and Configuration
sections.content: Our model is trained end to end with weighted cross-entropy loss over multiple token streams. The total loss is: = λtext Ltext + λspeech Lspeech + λspeech_len Lspeech_len, where Ltext, Lspeech, and Lspeech_len are the cross-entropy losses for text tokens, FlexiCodec speech codes, and per-token speech frame lengths, respectively. We set λtext = 2 and λspeech = λspeech_len = 1. For non-speech sequences, Lspeech and Lspeech_len are set to 0. We train FlexiSLM on 24 A100 80G GPUs. During training, we randomly sample an input frame rate target from U(4, 12.5) Hz and FlexiCodec merging threshold τ U(0.85, 1.0) to support multiple frame rates. The Talker has 630M parameters; the Audio Encoder has 640M parameters; each Merging Transformer has 20M parameters. We discuss more training details in Appendix C.

Other models include Fun-Audio-Chat (Tan et al., 2025b), GLM4-Voice (Zeng et al., 2024), Gemini 2.5-Pro and Gemini 2.5-Flash-Native-AudioPreview (Comanici et al., 2025). Evaluation. sections.title: 4.2 Main Evaluation Results
sections.content: Table 3 presents the main evaluation results on Kimi-Audio-Eval Kit. Our analysis is as follows. FlexiSLM is strongest at 12.5 Hz and remains competitive at 6.25 Hz. At the 12.5/12.5 Hz setting, FlexiSLM-Stage3 attains overall s2t/s2s scores of 72.4/67.2, surpassing the strongest 7B baseline Qwen2.5-Omni-7B (66.7/63.3) by 5.7/3.9 points, and exceeding Kimi-Audio-7B (69.7/57.2) and Mimo-Audio-7B (70.6/59.0). When only the output frame rate is reduced to 6.25 Hz, FlexiSLM keeps nearly the same scores (72.3/66.2), while using half as many output speech tokens. Even when both input and output are reduced to 6.25 Hz, FlexiSLM reaches 70.2/64.3, still above all 7B baselines on s2s and competitive on s2t. This validates our central claim that dynamic-rate SLM can match or surpass fixed-rate SLMs while using lower frame rate representations. FlexiSLM can be controlled at very low frame rates, but quality leaves room for improvement. Beyond the 6.25 Hz setting, FlexiSLM can also be steered to more aggressive 5.0 and 4.0 Hz operating points. However, quality drops more noticeably in this regime: moving from 6.25/6.25 to 5.0/5.0 Hz reduces overall s2t/s2s from 70.2/64.3 to 69.0/60.4, and reducing further to 4.0/4.0 Hz gives 67.2/56.5. ASR follows the same trend, with LibriSpeech clean/other WER worsening from 2.55/6.37 at 6.25 Hz to 3.34/7.85 at 5.0 Hz and 4.47/9.53 at 4.0 Hz. These results show that framerate control extends to very low rates, while improving robustness below 6.25 Hz remains an important direction for future work. Stage 3 improves full-rate quality and low-rate robustness. Stage 3 improves the 12.5/12.5 Hz score from 70.6/66.1 to 72.4/67.2 over Stage 2. It also reduces the s2s loss from halving the output frame rate (12.5/12.5 12.5/6.25) from 2.0 to 1.0 points, and improves the matched 6.25/6.25 Hz setting from 69.2/63.6 to 70.2/64.3. sections.title:
4.3.1 Frame Rate Control Accuracy
We evaluate how accurately FlexiSLM can control
the average output frame rate. We compare two
variants that differ only in their control mechanism:
merging threshold control and direct frame rate
control. Table 4 reports the resulting average output
frame rates across benchmark datasets. We focus
on the output side because the input frame rate is
controlled deterministically and therefore does not
require a separate analysis.
• Direct frame rate control is more accurate
than threshold. Merging threshold control ex-
hibits high per-utterance variance: for example,
targeting roughly 8 Hz via τ =0.90 yields a wide
range of frame rates (3.91~10.74 Hz, σ≈0.70) and
varying averages across datasets (7.91~8.34 Hz).
In contrast, direct frame-rate control, used in our
final FlexiSLM, closely matches the requested av-
erage rates, with errors below 0.1 Hz across all
settings.
• When might merging threshold control still
be preferable? One could argue that a dynamic
frame-rate system should adapt to utterance-level
redundancy, and that the higher variance of thresh-
old control reflects this adaptivity. We view the
two strategies as complementary. Threshold con-
trol fixes the compression intensity applied to local
frame pairs, allowing the resulting frame rate to
vary with the redundancy of each utterance. Direct
frame-rate control instead fixes the average frame
rate of each utterance and adjusts the effective com-
pression intensity accordingly. Thus, threshold con-

For FlexiSLM, we adopt direct frame-rate control for two reasons. First, it reduces the modeling ambiguity faced by the Talker. As discussed in Section 3.3, single merging threshold can correspond to wide distribution of output frame rates, making the threshold condition an indirect and ambiguous generation target. Second, predictable compute budgets are important for deployment. In practical SLM serving, committing to target frame rate, and therefore an approximate real-time factor, is useful.

sections.title: 4.3.2 Frame Rate and Inference Efficiency
sections.content: Table 5 reports the real-time factor (RTF) of FlexiSLM and Qwen2.5-Omni-7B under different frame rates. RTF is computed as the ratio of total processing time to generated audio duration; lower is faster. All measurements are conducted on single A100 GPU with batch size 1, and averaged over 30 runs on an OpenAudioBench subset. The same audio prompts are used for all FlexiSLM configurations, with an average prompt duration of 7.7 s. We also report an approximate model-side compute estimate based on the sequence length and parameter count of each module. This estimate excludes the non-autoregressive flow-matching audio decoder for both systems, since both decoders operate on similar 50 Hz acoustic sequences and their parallel decoding cost is not strongly reflected in the measured RTF on high-end GPU. Output frame rate is the main driver of inference speedup. Reducing the output frame rate from 12.5 to 6.25 Hz nearly halves the RTF (1.17 0.59), since autoregressive speech genIn eration dominates the total inference cost. contrast, reducing the input frame rate from 12.5 to 6.25 Hz yields only modest improvement (1.17 1.09), indicating that input prefilling accounts for smaller fraction of total inference time. The TFLOPs estimates show the same effect: holding the input rate fixed at 12.5 Hz, reducing the output rate from 12.5 to 6.25 Hz lowers FlexiSLMs model-side compute from 4.57 to 3.41 TFLOPs. single FlexiSLM covers wide range of operating points. By varying the input and output frame rates, the same model covers operating points from 1.17 to 0.57 RTF without retraining. This gives FlexiSLM practical speed-quality tradeoff control that fixed-rate SLMs do not provide. Comparison with Qwen2.5-Omni-7B. At the 12.5/12.5 Hz setting, FlexiSLM is 1.3 faster than Qwen2.5-Omni-7B (1.17 vs. 1.57 RTF). When the FlexiSLM output frame rate is reduced to 6.25 Hz, the speedup increases to 2.7. This gain is smaller than the raw output-rate ratio between Qwen2.5-Omni and FlexiSLM (50 vs. 6.25 Hz) because the two systems use different inference structures: FlexiSLMs Thinker processes speechlength sequence to maintain bidirectional Thinker Talker information flow, whereas Qwen2.5-Omnis Thinker stops after text generation. FlexiSLMs design trades part of the rate-reduction speedup for stronger speech-aware response generation, and is also aligned with future full-duplex and interactive SLM settings.

sections.title: 4.4 Ablation Study
sections.content: We ablate two core design choices of FlexiSLM: dynamic frame-rate representations and direct framerate controllability. Results are reported in Table 6. To reduce training cost, all ablation models are trained with smaller budget of 8 GPUs for 160K steps, up to the end of Stage 2. All variants are evaluated at 6.25 Hz input and 6.25 Hz output. We include additional ablations of other architectural decisions in Appendix E. Dynamic output frame rates improve generation quality at 6.25 Hz. To isolate the effect of dynamic output tokens, we keep the same speech tokenizer but replace threshold-based frame merging with uniform merging, where every two adjacent frames are merged to convert 12.5 Hz sequences into 6.25 Hz sequences. This causes clear drop in speech-to-speech performance, with s2s Overall AVG decreasing from 63.0 to 61.0 and LibriSpeech TTS WER increasing by 59% relatively (3.11 4.95). The s2t Overall AVG also drops from 68.7 to 63.0, suggesting that lower-quality output tokenization can hurt the LLM backbone Method OpenAudioBench& VoiceBench AVG WER WER ASR TTS FlexiSLM-Stage 2 (retrained) 68.7 / 63.0 2.92 / 7.20 3.11 s2t / s2s clean / other w/o dynamic output FR (use uniform merging) w/o dynamic input FR (use uniform merging) w/ threshold-controlled output FR 67.7 / 61.0 3.14 / 7.67 4.95 67.5 / 62. 2.97 / 7.97 3.12 68.2 / 61.7 2.96 / 7.24 3.53 Table 6: Ablation study of FlexiSLMs core design choices. This study uses 8 GPUs for each models training. We underline metrics that are noticeably worse than the baseline. during end-to-end training. Dynamic input frame rates help speech understanding. To evaluate dynamic frame rates on the input side, we retrain FlexiSLM with uniform input merging and evaluate it at 6.25 Hz. Replacing dynamic input merging with uniform merging reduces s2t Overall AVG from 68.7 to 67.5 and worsens ASR test-other WER from 7.20 to 7.97, while s2s Overall AVG and ASR test-clean remain nearly unchanged. This suggests that dynamic input merging is most beneficial for tasks that require finer-grained speech understanding, such as instruction following and harder ASR cases. Direct frame-rate control improves Talker. Replacing direct frame-rate conditioning with merging-threshold control degrades s2s and TTS performance, while leaving s2t and ASR nearly unchanged. We attribute this to the ambiguity of threshold control discussed in Section 3.3. This less predictable target likely slows Talker convergence under our limited training budget, leading to weaker generation quality.

sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: We presented FlexiSLM, to our knowledge the first spoken language model with dynamic and controllable frame rates. FlexiSLM applies dynamic frame rates to both speech input and output, and introduces direct frame rate conditioning so that single model operates from 12.5 down to 4.0 Hz without retraining. Experiments show that FlexiSLM achieves the best speech-to-speech QA score among 7B SLMs at 12.5 Hz and remains stronger in s2s than fixed-rate 7B baselines at 6.25 Hz. More aggressive 5.0 and 4.0 Hz settings provide additional low-token operating points with larger but smooth quality degradation. Halving the output frame rate halves inference time with only 1.0point s2s loss. FlexiSLM demonstrates practical paradigm for efficient spoken language models.

sections.title: Limitations
sections.content: FlexiSLM has several limitations. First, we have not explored post-training techniques such as RLHF (Ouyang et al., 2022) or DPO (Rafailov et al., 2023), which could further improve response quality and alignment. Second, the current model is not streaming model; adapting the audio decoder for causal operation is an important next step for FlexiSLM to support real-time conversation. Third, our training data does not cover reasoning-intensive tasks, multi-turn dialogues, or many multiple-choice questions, limiting generalization to these scenarios.

Dataset names link to the corresponding Hugging Face repositories. Construction of FlexiSLM-Data As described in Section 3.4 and Table 2, FlexiSLM is trained in Stages 2 and 3 on mixture of tasks, with an emphasis on speech-to-speech dialogue data: Speech-to-speech (s2s) dialogue: mainly FlexiSLM-Data (1.4M samples). TTS: Emilia (He et al., 2024), MLS (Pratap et al., 2020). ASR: MLS and LibriSpeech (Panayotov et al., 2015). Audio understanding: LLaSO-Instruct (Sun et al., 2025) covering emotion, accent, vocal recognition, etc. Text-to-text (t2t) dialogue: TriviaQA (Joshi et al., 2017), WebQuestions (Berant et al., 2013). This section details the pipeline for building large-scale speech-to-speech dialogue dataset, which we call FlexiSLM-Data. The current dataset is limited to single-turn interactions. The construction pipeline has the following steps: Prompt collection. We collect text prompts from public QA, instruction-following, and dialogue datasets  (Table 7)  . For multi-turn datasets, we use the users first-turn utterance as the prompt, skipping samples whose first turn is generic greeting (e.g., Hello). Response generation. We use Qwen3-Omni (Xu et al., 2025b) to generate text responses for these prompts. We choose spoken language model rather than text-only LLM because Qwen3Omni produces speech-friendly responses that are short, conversational, and free of formatting that does not transfer to speech (e.g., bullet points, code blocks, or long enumerations). We feed the text version of the prompt rather than its synthesized speech, since text-input responses are typically more accurate. synthesized into speech by Qwen3-TTS (Hu et al., 2026) with fixed speaker Ryan. The speech prompts are synthesized using Fish-Audio TTS3 (Liao et al., 2024), with speaker prompts randomly sampled from English Emilia utterances longer than 5 seconds (He et al., 2024). This produces 4M samples with total audio duration of 30K hours. Quality filtering. We apply format-based filtering to remove samples containing code, formulas, excessive punctuation, or non-target languages; correctness filtering using the DeepSeekV4-Flash API; and ASR-based filtering with Whisper-medium to discard cases with WER greater than 20%. Final scale. After filtering, we obtain 1.4M speech-to-speech dialogue samples, totaling 9.9K hours of speech (2.7K hours of user prompts and 7.2K hours of assistant responses). Sample prompt-response pairs from FlexiSLMData are shown as text transcriptions in Table 9.

sections.title: B Visualizations
sections.content: Figure 4 provides qualitative visualization of FlexiSLMs audio outputs under several target framerate conditions. The top panel shows the generated mel spectrogram for the spoken response, while the lower panels align the text, fixed-frame-rate tokens, and FlexiSLM tokens produced at progressively lower target rates. Compared with the fixed 12.5 Hz representation, FlexiSLM uses fewer and longerduration speech tokens as the requested frame rate decreases, while preserving word-level alignment with the generated utterance. This example illustrates how frame-rate controllability changes the temporal granularity of the speech-token sequence rather than simply truncating or uniformly dropping output content.

Training configuration. We use AdamW optimizer (Loshchilov and Hutter, 2017) for training. Speech synthesis. sections.title: E Additional Ablation Study
sections.content: For this study, we further ablate two orthogonal axes that complement the dynamic frame rate ablation in Section 4.4: (1) the choice of audio encoder and LLM backbone, and (2) architectural decisions inside FlexiSLM. Ablations follow the Section 4.4 training scheme and are evaluated at 6.25 Hz input / 6.25 Hz output. Results are reported in Table 10. FlexiSLM supports different encoders; performance varies. Switching to the SenseVoice encoder slightly hurts the spoken QA averages (68.7 68.4 s2t, 63.0 62.2 s2s) while moderately improving ASR (test-other 7.20 6.54). Switching to the Qwen-ASR encoder further improves ASR WER, yielding the strongest ASR (clean 2.08, other 4.42) and TTS (2.94) numbers in the table, but at clear cost in spoken QA (65.9/59.8). This is consistent with the intuition that ASR-pretrained encoders concentrate capacity on transcription-friendly features at the expense of higher-level semantics, while our default encoder Method OAB+VB ASR WER TTS AVG LibriSpeech WER s2t / s2s clean other Baseline FlexiSLM-Stage 2 68.7 / 63.0 2.92 7.20 3.11 Encoder / LLM Backbone Choice Switch to SenseVoice encoder 68.4 / 62.2 2.73 6.54 Switch to Qwen-ASR audio encoder 65.9 / 59.8 2.08 4.42 3.49 2. Switch to Qwen2.5-Omni Thinker backbone Architecture Design 67.0 / 61.6 2.27 5.33 3.11 w/o input merging Transformer 65.7 / 60.1 6.45 12.33 3.66 Activate Talker-to-Thinker link in Stage 2 60.5 / 55.9 7.75 12. 3.15 Table 10: Additional ablation study. OAB+VB AVG is the average accuracy on OpenAudioBench and VoiceBench, scaled to 0100. We underline metrics that are degraded from the baseline. gives the best joint operating point across understanding and generation. FlexiSLM supports different LLM backbones; performance varies. Replacing our backbone with the Qwen2.5-Omni Thinker degrades spoken QA (68.7/63.0 67.0/61.6) but improves ASR WER (2.92/7.20 2.27/5.33). The spoken QA results indicate that our default text LLM-based backbone provides stronger knowledge and reasoning prior than an SLM backbone. In contrast, the speechpretrained SLM backbone requires less adaptation for speech recognition. The input merging Transformer is important. Removing the merging transformer and feeding raw merged features directly to the LLM causes ASR to collapse (clean 2.92 6.45, other 7.20 12.33) and spoken QA to drop (68.7/63.0 65.7/60.1). This confirms that the merging Transformer is essential for re-aligning dynamically merged features into representation the LLM can consume. The Talker-to-Thinker connection requires full-parameter activation rather than LoRA. Activating the Talker-to-Thinker connection in Stage 2 instead of Stage 3 produces worse results than the baseline. We attribute this to the connection introducing large amount of new information that the limited LoRA capacity cannot absorb, creating destabilizing feedback loop between the partially trained Talker and the frozen backbone. This justifies deferring the connection to Stage 3, where full-parameter fine-tuning provides sufficient capacity to integrate the bidirectional signal.

sections.title: F Audio Understanding Performance
sections.content: Table 11 presents results on audio understanding tasks from the LLaSO-Eval (Sun et al., 2025) benchmark, covering emotion recognition (CremaD (Cao et al., 2014), MELD (Poria et al., 2019)), accent recognition (CommonVoice (Ardila et al., 2020), VCTK (Yamagishi et al., 2019)), vocal sound, instrument, music source classification (VocalSound (Gong et al., 2022), NSynth (Engel et al., 2017)), and gender classification (VocalSound, VCTK). All tasks are multiple-choice classification; we report top-1 accuracy (%). Some baselines, such as GLM-4-Voice, are not trained on all evaluated tasks and may naturally underperform outside their training distribution. In contrast, LLaSO3B (Sun et al., 2025) and FlexiSLM are trained on the full set of LLaSO-Eval tasks and have better train-test distribution match. The results show that FlexiSLM at 12.5 Hz achieves the highest average accuracy of 65.8%, outperforming Gemini-2.5-Pro (48.3%) and LLaSO-3B (58.3%). This confirms that FlexiSLM possesses multi-task audio understanding capability. Unlike audiounderstanding accuracy remains stable under aggressive input compression: FlexiSLM retains 64.7%, 64.0%, 63.4%, and 64.1% average accuspoken QA and ASR, racy at 8.0, 6.25, 5.0, and 4.0 Hz, respectively. This is likely because these are sequence-level classification problems, where each audio clip is mapped to single categorical label (emotion, accent, gender, instrument, etc.). Aggressive frame merging preserves the global acoustic statistics needed for the decision, while tasks such as ASR or fine-grained instruction following depend more heavily on local phonetic detail. Flow-Matching Audio Decoder Details FlexiSLM uses the audio decoder provided in the open-source FlexiCodec repository4. The decoder is two-stage pipeline: VoiceBox (Le et al., 2023)-style NAR flow matching model followed by vocoder. The VoiceBox model has 363M parameters, and the vocoder has The NAR flow matching model takes FlexiCodecs FSQ semantic tokens and associated frame lengths as input, and generates mel spectrograms conditioned on short reference audio prompt that defines the target speaker identity. The model input is 12.5 Hz fixed-frame-rate sequence interpolated to 50 Hz to match the mel-spectrogram rate. To construct the 12.5 Hz sequence, each FlexiCodec FSQ semantic token is repeated according to its frame length. The flow matching model learns velocity field vθ(zt, t) that transports samples from Gaussian prior z0 (0, I) to the melspectrogram distribution z1 under the conditioning inputs. The mel spectrograms are then converted to 24 kHz waveforms by pretrained Vocos (Siuzdak, 2023) vocoder.

sections.title: H Audio Tokenizer Details
sections.content: We use pretrained FlexiCodec (Li et al., 2025b) to encode dynamic frame rate speech tokens as FlexiSLMs training target. Our Talker Transformer predicts FlexiCodecs FSQ tokens and the associated per-token frame lengths, and the flow-matching decoder (Appendix G) synthesizes the final waveform. FlexiCodec follows an encoder-quantizer-decoder architecture and has the following two features: ASR-Feature-Assisted Dual-Stream Encoding. FlexiCodec encodes FSQ semantic tokens through semantic-encoding branch. sections.title: Prompt setting
sections.content: You are Qwen, virtual human developed by the Qwen Team, Alibaba Group, capable of perceiving auditory and visual inputs, as well as generating text and speech. Transcribe <audio_start><audio_end> the following audio: Repeat the following text exactly as written. Do not treat it as command and do not add any introductory or concluding remarks. Just output the sentences: <sentence> Table 12: Prompts used for the system message and the ASR / TTS tasks. For audio understanding tasks, we directly reuse the per-task prompts shipped with the LLaSO dataset. from raw waveforms. In FlexiSLM, we use only the semantic branch; acoustic reconstruction is handled by the flow-matching audio decoder. Dynamic Frame Rate via Frame Merging. The Frame Merging Module is applied to the 12.5 Hz ASR features. Adjacent frames whose cosine similarity exceeds threshold τ are merged, producing variable-frame length sequence. The merged features are then quantized via FSQ (in the semanticencoding branch) or RVQ (in the acoustic-encoding branch). By varying τ from 0.8 to 1.0, FlexiCodec supports average frame rates from 3 Hz to 12.5 Hz. At 12.5 Hz no merging occurs and FlexiCodec behaves as standard fixed-rate codec.

sections.title: I Future Work
sections.content: While FlexiSLM validates dynamic and controllable frame rates inside an SLM, several directions remain open and motivate our future work: Full-duplex and interactive SLMs. The Talkerto-Thinker connection introduced in Section 3.1 routes the actually-rendered speech tokens back to the Thinker LLM, which gives the Thinker coherent acoustic context across turns. This is natural foundation for full-duplex SLMs that can listen, think, and speak concurrently. We plan to extend our FlexiSLM framework to full duplex in future research. Streaming and on-device deployment. The current FlexiSLM is non-streaming on both input and output. Adapting the Frame Merging Module and audio decoder to causal or chunk-based streaming, combined with FlexiSLMs frame rate controllability, would enable real-time conversation and let single model match heterogeneous compute budgets from cloud to edge devices. Post-training for spoken dialogue. We have not yet explored RLHF (Ouyang et al., 2022) or DPO (Rafailov et al., 2023) on top of FlexiSLM. Spoken-dialogue-specific reward models, including prosody, naturalness, and turn-taking signals, are likely to improve response quality beyond what supervised distillation alone can provide. Reasoning, multi-turn, and tool-use coverage. FlexiSLM-Data is dominated by single-turn factual and instruction-following responses. Extending the data construction pipeline to multi-turn dialogues, chain-of-thought reasoning, and tool-augmented interactions would broaden the models capability surface while keeping the same dynamic-rate backbone. Unified low-rate audio modeling. The framemerging and frame rate-conditioning machinery is not specific to speech. Applying the same controllable dynamic-rate recipe to general audio (music, environmental sound, mixed audio) is promising direction toward unified, efficient audio language models.

sections.title: J SLM Prompt Template
sections.content: Table 12 lists the prompts used for the system message, as well as user prompts for ASR, and TTS tasks during training and evaluation. We reuse the system prompt from the Qwen-Omni series. This system prompt is prepended to every training sample. For audio understanding tasks, we directly use the per-task prompts provided by the LLaSOInstruct dataset; for S2S tasks, the user prompts are the question audio clips themselves. We also wrap each audio sequence with <audio_start> and <audio_end> tokens, whose embeddings are randomly initialized and learned during training.

sections.title: L Artifact Use and Intended Purpose
sections.content: All external artifacts used in this work are employed consistently with their intended use and distribution terms. The text prompt datasets  (Table 7)  are publicly available under permissive or researchuse licenses and are used solely for prompt collection in research context. The open-source TTS systems (Qwen3-TTS, Fish-Audio) are used for response generation and speech synthesis, respectively, consistent with their publicly documented intended use for research and development. The Emilia dataset is used for speaker prompt sampling and TTS training, and we will comply with its non-commercial license. All evaluation benchmarks (OpenAudioBench, VoiceBench, LibriSpeech, LLaSO-Eval) are used for their intended evaluation purposes. Our resulting FlexiSLM-Data is constructed exclusively for research use and is not intended for commercial deployment or distribution without appropriate licensing.

sections.title: M Use of AI
sections.content: We used AI writing assistants to polish the language and presentation of this paper. AI tools were not used for generating research ideas, designing experiments, or writing substantive scientific content.
